### Post by k0tt0n on 2008-08-16
on boot up today i get the message "Cannot Mount Volume" "You are not privileged to mount the volume" when i try to open my internal NTFS drives/partitions in nautilis. i dual boot with XP but have not had this occur before. 
my fstab appears to be the same.

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sdc5 :
UUID=78abc143-3c0b-4657-b19a-1f61fbd4c1da / ext3 relatime,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sdc6 :
UUID=05da5fed-f792-46c3-bcf6-a96a43fca420 none swap sw 0 0
/dev/scd1 /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/scd0 /media/cdrom1 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/sdc1 /media/Windows\040Drive ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_AU.UTF-8 0 0
/dev/sdb1 /media/E\040Partition ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_AU.UTF-8 0 0
/dev/sda1 /media/Music ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_AU.UTF-8 0 0

thanks

### Post by dexter on 2008-08-17
Try this command, it should mount everything in fstab:
```
sudo mount -a
```

### Post by k0tt0n on 2008-08-17
no luck.

$LogFile indicates unclean shutdown (0, 0)
Failed to mount '/dev/sdc1': Operation not supported
Mount is denied because NTFS is marked to be in use. Choose one action:

Choice 1: If you have Windows then disconnect the external devices by
          clicking on the 'Safely Remove Hardware' icon in the Windows
          taskbar then shutdown Windows cleanly.

Choice 2: If you don't have Windows then you can use the 'force' option for
          your own responsibility. For example type on the command line:

            mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sdc1 /media/Windows Drive -o force

    Or add the option to the relevant row in the /etc/fstab file:

            /dev/sdc1 /media/Windows Drive ntfs-3g force 0 0
$LogFile indicates unclean shutdown (0, 1)
Failed to mount '/dev/sdb1': Operation not supported
Mount is denied because NTFS is marked to be in use. Choose one action:

Choice 1: If you have Windows then disconnect the external devices by
          clicking on the 'Safely Remove Hardware' icon in the Windows
          taskbar then shutdown Windows cleanly.

Choice 2: If you don't have Windows then you can use the 'force' option for
          your own responsibility. For example type on the command line:

            mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sdb1 /media/E Partition -o force

    Or add the option to the relevant row in the /etc/fstab file:

            /dev/sdb1 /media/E Partition ntfs-3g force 0 0
$LogFile indicates unclean shutdown (0, 0)
Failed to mount '/dev/sda1': Operation not supported
Mount is denied because NTFS is marked to be in use. Choose one action:

Choice 1: If you have Windows then disconnect the external devices by
          clicking on the 'Safely Remove Hardware' icon in the Windows
          taskbar then shutdown Windows cleanly.

Choice 2: If you don't have Windows then you can use the 'force' option for
          your own responsibility. For example type on the command line:

            mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /media/Music -o force

    Or add the option to the relevant row in the /etc/fstab file:

            /dev/sda1 /media/Music ntfs-3g force 0 0

---

### Post by WRDN on 2008-08-17
That message appears if you did a "dirty" shutdown of Windows, and it was not able to unmount the partition(s) properly. Either boot into Windows, and shutdown properly, or run the command:

```
mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sdc1 "/media/Windows Drive" -o force
```

---

### Post by k0tt0n on 2008-08-17
> **WRDN said:**
> That message appears if you did a "dirty" shutdown of Windows, and it was not able to unmount the partition(s) properly. Either boot into Windows, and shutdown properly, or run the command:

it was as simple as booting into windows and shutting down cleanly. thanks for that WRDN
